title: Git history fallback assumes .beads is direct child of repo root
description:
## Problem

`checkGitHistoryForDeletions` assumes the repo structure:

```go
repoRoot := filepath.Dir(beadsDir)  // Assumes .beads is in repo root
jsonlPath := filepath.Join(".beads", "beads.jsonl")
```

But `.beads` could be in a subdirectory (monorepo, nested project), and the actual JSONL filename could be different (configured via `metadata.json`).

## Location
`internal/importer/importer.go:865-869`

## Impact
- Git search will fail silently for repos with non-standard structure
- Monorepo users won't get deletion propagation

## Fix
1. Use `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` to find actual repo root
2. Compute relative path from repo root to JSONL
3. Or use `git -C <dir>` to run from beadsDir directly

title: bd preflight: PR readiness checks for contributors
description:
## Vision

Encode project-specific institutional knowledge into executable checks. CONTRIBUTING.md is documentation that's read once and forgotten; `bd preflight` is documentation that runs at exactly the right moment.

## Problem Statement

Contributors face a "last mile" problem - they do the work but stumble on project-specific gotchas at PR time:
- Nix vendorHash gets stale when go.sum changes
- Beads artifacts leak into PRs (see bd-umbf for namespace solution)
- Version mismatches between version.go and default.nix
- Tests/lint not run locally before pushing
- Other project-specific checks that only surface when CI fails

These are too obscure to remember, exist in docs nobody reads end-to-end, and waste CI round-trips.

## Why beads?

Beads already has a foothold in the contributor workflow. It knows:
- Git state (staged files, branch, dirty status)
- Project structure
- The specific issue being worked on
- Project-specific configuration

## Proposed Interface

### Tier 1: Checklist Mode (v1)

    $ bd preflight
    PR Readiness Checklist:

    [ ] Tests pass: go test -short ./...
    [ ] Lint passes: golangci-lint run ./...
    [ ] No beads pollution: check .beads/issues.jsonl diff
    [ ] Nix hash current: go.sum unchanged or vendorHash updated
    [ ] Version sync: version.go matches default.nix

    Run 'bd preflight --check' to validate automatically.

### Tier 2: Check Mode (v2)

    $ bd preflight --check
    ✓ Tests pass
    ✓ Lint passes
    ⚠ Beads pollution: 3 issues in diff - are these project issues or personal?
    ✗ Nix hash stale: go.sum changed, vendorHash needs update
      Fix: sha256-KRR6dXzsSw8OmEHGBEVDBOoIgfoZ2p0541T9ayjGHlI=
    ✓ Version sync

    1 error, 1 warning. Run 'bd preflight --fix' to auto-fix where possible.

### Tier 3: Fix Mode (v3)

    $ bd preflight --fix
    ✓ Updated vendorHash in default.nix
    ⚠ Cannot auto-fix beads pollution - manual review needed

## Checks to Implement

| Check | Description | Auto-fixable |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| tests | Run go test -short ./... | No |
| lint | Run golangci-lint | Partial (gofmt) |
| beads-pollution | Detect personal issues in diff | No (see bd-umbf) |
| nix-hash | Detect stale vendorHash | Yes (if nix available) |
| version-sync | version.go matches default.nix | Yes |
| no-debug | No TODO/FIXME/console.log | Warn only |
| clean-stage | No unintended files staged | Warn only |

## Future: Configuration

Make checks configurable per-project via .beads/preflight.yaml:

    preflight:
      checks:
        - name: tests
          run: go test -short ./...
          required: true
        - name: no-secrets
          pattern: "**/*.env"
          staged: deny
        - name: custom-check
          run: ./scripts/validate.sh

This lets any project using beads define their own preflight checks.

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Static Checklist
- Implement bd preflight with hardcoded checklist for beads
- No execution, just prints what to check
- Update CONTRIBUTING.md to reference it

### Phase 2: Automated Checks
- Implement bd preflight --check
- Run tests, lint, detect stale hashes
- Clear pass/fail/warn output

### Phase 3: Auto-fix
- Implement bd preflight --fix
- Fix vendorHash, version sync
- Integrate with bd-umbf solution for pollution

### Phase 4: Configuration
- .beads/preflight.yaml support
- Make it useful for other projects using beads
- Plugin/hook system for custom checks

## Dependencies

- bd-umbf: Namespace isolation for beads pollution (blocking for full solution)

## Success Metrics

- Fewer CI failures on first PR push
- Reduced "fix nix hash" commits
- Contributors report preflight caught issues before CI

title: Fix `bd sync` failure due to daemon auto-export timestamp skew
description:

Proposed Fixes:
1. `bd sync` auto-imports if timestamp matches but content differs (or just auto-imports).
2. Content-based comparison instead of timestamp only.

title: bd import silently succeeds when database matches working tree but not git HEAD
description:
**Critical**: bd import reports '0 created, 0 updated' when database matches working tree JSONL, even when working tree is ahead of git HEAD. This gives false confidence that everything is synced with the source of truth.

## Reproduction

1. Start with database synced to working tree .beads/issues.jsonl (376 issues)
2. Git HEAD has older version of .beads/issues.jsonl (354 issues)
3. Run: bd import .beads/issues.jsonl
4. Output: 'Import complete: 0 created, 0 updated'

## Problem

User expects 'bd import' after 'git pull' to sync database with committed state, but:
- Command silently succeeds because DB already matches working tree
- No warning that working tree has uncommitted changes
- User falsely believes everything is synced with git
- Violates 'JSONL in git is source of truth' principle

## Expected Behavior

When .beads/issues.jsonl differs from git HEAD, bd import should:
1. Detect uncommitted changes: git diff --quiet HEAD .beads/issues.jsonl
2. Warn user: 'Warning: .beads/issues.jsonl has uncommitted changes (376 lines vs 354 in HEAD)'
3. Clarify status: 'Import complete: 0 created, 0 updated (already synced with working tree)'
4. Recommend: 'Run git diff .beads/issues.jsonl to review uncommitted work'

## Impact

- Users can't trust 'bd import' status messages
- Silent data loss risk if user assumes synced and runs git checkout
- Breaks mental model of 'JSONL in git = source of truth'
- Critical for VC's landing-the-plane workflow

title: Fix remaining FK constraint failures in AddComment and ApplyCompaction
description:
Follow-up to PR #348 (Fix FOREIGN KEY constraint failed).

The initial fix addressed CloseIssue, UpdateIssueID, and RemoveLabel.
However, `AddComment` (in internal/storage/sqlite/events.go) and `ApplyCompaction` (in internal/storage/sqlite/compact.go) still suffer from the same pattern: inserting an event after an UPDATE without verifying the UPDATE affected any rows.

This causes "FOREIGN KEY constraint failed" errors when operating on non-existent issues, instead of clean "issue not found" errors.

Task:
1. Apply the same fix pattern to `AddComment` and `ApplyCompaction`: check RowsAffected() after UPDATE and before event INSERT.
2. Ensure error messages are consistent ("issue %s not found").
3. Verify with reproduction tests (create a test that calls these methods with a non-existent ID).

title: Implicit deletion cases do not check for tombstones in remaining side
description:
In merge3WayWithTTL(), the implicit deletion cases (lines 426-435) handle when an issue exists in base and one side but not the other. The current logic does not check if the remaining side is a tombstone:

Case: inBase && inLeft && !inRight (deleted implicitly in right)
- If leftIssue is a tombstone, we should keep it (tombstone propagation)
- Currently: continue (drops the tombstone)

Case: inBase && !inLeft && inRight (deleted implicitly in left)
- If rightIssue is a tombstone, we should keep it
- Currently: continue (drops the tombstone)

This could cause tombstones to be lost during merge if one side has a tombstone and the other side implicitly deleted. The tombstone should win since its an explicit deletion record.

Files: internal/merge/merge.go:426-435

title: Implement global N-way collision resolution algorithm
description:
**Summary:** Replaced pairwise collision resolution with a global N-way algorithm that deterministically resolves issue ID conflicts across multiple clones. The new approach groups collisions, deduplicates by content hash, and assigns sequential IDs to ensure consistent synchronization.

**Key Decisions:**
- Use content hash for global, stable sorting
- Group collisions by base ID
- Assign sequential IDs based on sorted unique versions
- Eliminate order-dependent remapping logic

**Resolution:** Implemented ResolveNWayCollisions function that guarantees deterministic issue ID assignment across multiple synchronization scenarios, solving the core challenge of maintaining consistency in distributed systems with potential conflicts.

Fix: bd migrate-tombstones corrupts deletions manifest
description:
When running 'bd migrate-tombstones' followed by 'bd sync', the operation adds thousands of issues to deletions.jsonl with author 'bd-doctor-hydrate' that should never have been deleted. This causes:
1. Issues are marked as deleted that were never intended for deletion
2. Subsequent 'bd sync' fails with 3-way merge errors
3. The migration operation corrupts database state

## Root Cause
The tombstone migration logic incorrectly identifies issues that should be migrated as 'deleted issues' and adds them to the deletions manifest instead of converting them to inline tombstones.

## Reproduction
1. bd sync --import-only
2. bd migrate-tombstones
3. bd sync
Result: See thousands of 'Skipping bd-xxxx (in deletions manifest: deleted 2025-12-14 by bd-doctor-hydrate)' lines and 3-way merge failures

## Impact
- Database becomes inconsistent
- Issues permanently lost from local database
- Requires full reset to recover

## Files to Investigate
- cmd/bd/migrate_tombstones.go - migration logic
- deletions manifest handling during migration

title: Implement content-hash based collision resolution for deterministic convergence
description:
The current collision resolution uses creation timestamps to decide which issue to keep vs. remap. This is non-deterministic when two clones create issues at nearly the same time.

Root cause of bd-71107098:
- Clone A creates test-1="Issue from clone A" at T0
- Clone B creates test-1="Issue from clone B" at T0+30ms
- Clone B syncs first, remaps Clone A's to test-2
- Clone A syncs second, sees collision, remaps Clone B's to test-2
- Result: titles are swapped between clones

Solution:
- Use content-based hashing (title + description + priority + type)
- Deterministic winner: always keep issue with lower hash
- Same collision on different clones produces same result (idempotent)

Implementation:
- Modify ScoreCollisions in internal/storage/sqlite/collision.go
- Replace timestamp-based scoring with content hash comparison
- Ensure hash function is stable across platforms

title: Configure database connection pool limits for daemon mode
description:
Database connection pool not configured for file-based databases when running in daemon mode.

Location: internal/storage/sqlite/sqlite.go:108-116

Problem:
- Daemon is a long-running server handling concurrent RPC requests
- Multiple CLI commands hit same daemon simultaneously  
- Go default: unlimited connections (MaxOpenConns=0)
- SQLite IMMEDIATE transactions serialize on write lock
- Can have 100+ goroutines blocked waiting, each holding connection
- Results in connection exhaustion and 'database is locked' errors

Current code only limits in-memory DBs:
if isInMemory {
    db.SetMaxOpenConns(1)
    db.SetMaxIdleConns(1)
}
// File DBs: UNLIMITED connections!

Fix:
if !isInMemory {
    maxConns := runtime.NumCPU() + 1  // 1 writer + N readers
    db.SetMaxOpenConns(maxConns)
    db.SetMaxIdleConns(2)
    db.SetConnMaxLifetime(0)
}

Impact: 'database is locked' errors under concurrent load in daemon mode

Note: NOT an issue for direct CLI usage (each process isolated). Only affects daemon mode where multiple CLI commands share one database pool.

Effort: 1 hour

title: CLI tests (cli_fast_test.go) are slow and should be integration tests
description:
The TestCLI_* tests in cmd/bd/cli_fast_test.go are taking 4-5 seconds each (40+ seconds total), making them the slowest part of the fast test suite.

Current timings:
- TestCLI_Import: 4.73s
- TestCLI_Blocked: 4.33s  
- TestCLI_DepTree: 4.15s
- TestCLI_Close: 3.59s
- TestCLI_DepAdd: 3.50s
- etc.

These tests compile the bd binary once in init(), but then execute it multiple times per test with filesystem operations. Despite being named "fast", they're actually end-to-end CLI integration tests.

Options:
1. Tag with //go:build integration (move to integration suite)
2. Optimize: Use in-memory databases, reduce exec calls, better parallelization
3. Keep as-is but understand they're the baseline for "fast" tests

Total test suite currently: 13.8s (cmd/bd alone is 12.8s, and most of that is these CLI tests)
notes:
Fixed by reusing existing bd binary from repo root instead of rebuilding.

Before: 15+ minutes (rebuilding binary for every test package)
After: ~12 seconds (reuses pre-built binary)

The init() function now checks for ../../bd first before falling back to building. This means `go build && go test` is now fast.

title: Beads Orchestra: Workflow Orchestration for AI Agent Coordination
description:
Transform beads from a passive issue tracker into an active AI agent coordinator.

Automatic work dispatch when dependencies resolve, built-in retry/timeout handling, intelligent load balancing across agent capabilities, and full execution observability.

## Architecture

- Uses existing custom tables pattern (documented in EXTENDING.md)
- Runs within existing daemon infrastructure

## Phases

0. Foundation (Week 1) - Schema and package setup
1. Core Engine (Week 2-3) - Orchestration loop implementation
2. CLI Integration (Week 3-4) - User-facing commands
3. Dispatcher (Week 4-5) - Protocol implementations
4. Observability (Week 5-6) - Polish and production-ready features

## Success Metrics

- 99%+ execution success rate
- <100ms dispatch latency (p95)
- NPS > 50
